The segment ends with a split screen showing PUMA and adidas logos over American and German flags, and a silhouette of a man raising his arm in triumph against a stock market backdrop, symbolizing success and market dominance.

He is then seen at the University of Oregon, cutting open a shoe, with the text 'CUT THEM OPEN MAKE ADJUSTMENTS.' A runner on a track is shown in pain, with the text 'JUST MADE THEIR FEET PAINFUL,' contrasting with a later shot of a woman running comfortably, with the text 'IMPROVED THE SHOES BETTER OR MORE COMFORTABLE.' The segment ends with a man in a white shirt and cap, smiling, as the word 'BETTER' appears, symbolizing successful innovation.

segments.caption: The video segment opens with a purple Nike sneaker prominently displayed, overlaid with the text 'THEY DIDN’T TAKE PHIL SERIOUSLY,' setting a tone of initial skepticism. A diagram appears, showing a man’s face (Phil Knight) connected to three stores—two marked with red Xs, indicating rejection, while one labeled 'Runner Store' remains unmarked, suggesting a narrow path forward. The narrative shifts to a sepia-toned scene of Phil, now with white hair, holding a shoe, as the text 'SELL THESE SHOES' appears, emphasizing his determination. The title 'CHAPTER 4: BLUERIBBON' emerges, surrounded by sketches of sneakers, marking a new phase. The story progresses to Phil selling shoes from the trunk of his car, with a Kmart sign in the background, and the text 'STARTED SELLING THE SHOES FROM THE TRUNK OF HIS CAR' reinforcing his grassroots approach. A montage of runners on a track appears, with the text 'TO RUNNERS,' followed by a smiling man in sunglasses and the bold claim 'SELL OUT ALL 300 PAIRS WITHIN JUST 3 MONTHS,' highlighting early success. The scene transitions to a man in a collared shirt, looking determined, with the text 'SELLING ENCYCLOPAEDIAS DOOR TO DOOR' and 'STRANGELY EASY,' drawing a parallel to his sales experience. A close-up of a hand holding a purple Nike sneaker appears, with the text 'HIS PERSONAL BELIEF IN THE PRODUCT' and 'MADE PEOPLE WANNA BUY THEM,' underscoring authenticity. segments.caption: The video opens with a dynamic sequence featuring a white silhouette of a man against a green, money-filled background, overlaid with the Shopify logo, transitioning into a vibrant purple neon outline of the same silhouette, symbolizing digital entrepreneurship. This shifts to a close-up of hands counting money near a laptop, emphasizing financial success, followed by a green gradient screen with the Shopify logo and a call to action: 'CLICK THE LINK IN THE DESCRIPTION' with downward arrows, reinforcing the platform’s accessibility. The narrative then transitions to a dramatic title card: 'CHAPTER 5: BETRAYAL,' set against a backdrop of sneaker sketches, introducing a pivotal moment in the story. The scene cuts to a split-screen with the Man with White Hair on the left and the Man in Suit on the right, holding an Onitsuka Tiger shoe, as text overlays question whether Blue Ribbon Sports could become the exclusive distributor for Tiger shoes across America. A map of North America appears, with the U.S. highlighted in orange, and the word 'NO!' flashes, indicating rejection. The scene shifts to towering skyscrapers under an orange sky, with text explaining the reason: 'BECAUSE THEY DIDN'T THINK PHIL'S COMPANY WAS BIG OR ESTABLISHED ENOUGH TO HANDLE THAT.' A world map then appears, with the U.S. highlighted in yellow, and the Man with White Hair reappears, stating 'BUT WE DO HAVE OFFICES ON THE EAST COAST,' followed by a bold red 'THEY DIDN'T' to emphasize the contradiction. The Man in Suit is shown again, with the text 'ONITSUKA BELIEVED PHIL' and the Blue Ribbon Sports logo, affirming the partnership. The final frames show the Man with White Hair with a large '20,000' and a pair of Onitsuka Tiger shoes, symbolizing the successful sales milestone, as the narrative builds toward a climax of triumph and betrayal.

segments.caption: The segment opens with a purple-tinted map of the United States, overlaid with location pins and shipping boxes, symbolizing a distribution network. The scene transitions to a man in a suit, likely Phil Knight, in a dimly lit office, as promotional flyers for 'Blue Ribbon Sports' appear with bold text: 'INVEST IN BRS NOW!' The flyers are shown in a stack, emphasizing a campaign to raise capital. On-screen text reveals the company was described as a fast-growing shoe business seeking expansion, but the response was nonexistent—'NOBODY AT ALL RESPONDED'—leading to a moment of humiliation, visually represented by a man covering his face. The narrative shifts to a couple in a modest home, counting cash, with text indicating it was their 'ENTIRE LIFE SAVINGS'—$95,000—highlighted in front of a bank facade. The couple is then shown in an office meeting, where the man in a plaid shirt, identified as Jeff Johnson, appears frustrated and later in a backyard, visibly distressed. The segment ends with a split-screen of two older men, one with white hair and a mustache, the other with a stern expression, possibly representing the founders or key figures in the company’s early struggles, with a white Onitsuka Tiger sneaker superimposed between them, linking the financial hardship to the product.

segments.caption: The segment opens with a dimly lit office scene where a man in a suit, identified as the Man in Suit, is seen in a tense meeting, reflecting the growing strain in the business relationship. The narrative shifts to a series of animated graphics: a stack of 'SHOE DISTRIBUTOR' documents with a sneaker icon, followed by a calendar labeled 'APPOINTMENTS,' symbolizing the logistical and scheduling efforts of Blue Ribbon Sports. A man in a suit, likely a key Onitsuka executive, is shown in front of a chalkboard filled with diagrams and notes, with text overlay stating 'IT WAS NOW CLEAR THAT ONITSUKA WAS SECRETLY PLOTTING TO REPLACE BLUE RIBBON,' revealing the betrayal. A map of the USA in orange appears, with the Onitsuka Tiger logo, emphasizing the brand's dominance and the conflict over distribution. The text 'THANKS TO BLUE RIBBON'S EFFORT' appears, followed by silhouettes of executives with the message 'THE ONITSUKA EXECUTIVES DECIDED THAT THEY NEEDED TO MOVE ON TO EXPAND,' indicating their strategic shift. A close-up of a man in a blue shirt, possibly Phil Knight, shows him in deep thought, reflecting the emotional weight of the situation. The narrative escalates with the Onitsuka Tiger logo and text stating 'EITHER WE BUY 51% OF BLUE RIBBON SPORTS OR WE MAKE DEALS WITH OTHER DISTRIBUTION PARTNERS,' presenting the ultimatum. The Man in Suit is shown in a dimly lit setting, with text 'PHIL KNIGHT IMMEDIATELY REJECTED THE OFFER TO BUY HIS COMPANY,' highlighting his defiance. The scene transitions to a handshake graphic between Onitsuka Tiger and distribution partners, with a man in a suit holding an Onitsuka Tiger shoe, symbolizing the brand's new alliances. The final frame shows a fiery explosion with the text 'THEN IT WAS TIME FOR WAR,' marking the beginning of a direct confrontation between Onitsuka and Blue Ribbon Sports. The lighting throughout is predominantly purple and dark, creating a dramatic and tense atmosphere, with text overlays in bold yellow and orange to emphasize key plot points.

segments.caption: The segment opens with a bold title card: 'CHAPTER 6: DON’T GET ANGRY, GET EVEN,' set against a dark background with faint sneaker sketches, establishing a tone of retaliation and strategic resolve. The scene shifts to a dimly lit office where a Man in Suit stands by a window, gazing at a cityscape, his silhouette conveying contemplation and determination. The atmosphere is tense, underscored by purple and blue lighting. A quick cut to a man in a white shirt and tie, possibly a business associate, speaking earnestly in a dimly lit room, suggests a pivotal conversation. The narrative then transitions to a long, shadowy shoe warehouse with rows of illuminated shelves, evoking a sense of scale and inventory. A close-up of a white Onitsuka Tiger sneaker with blue and red stripes appears, symbolizing the product at the heart of the conflict. This is followed by a vintage storefront labeled 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS TIGER SHOES,' reinforcing the brand’s early identity. A cargo ship sails across a purple-tinted sea, overlaid with text 'AND SIMPLY IMPORTING WHATEVER WAS LEFT OVER TO THE US,' highlighting the distribution dispute. The scene cuts to a boardroom meeting where a man in a brown suit gestures passionately, surrounded by colleagues, indicating a strategic discussion. A woman hands a man a receipt labeled 'World's Toughest,' suggesting a transaction or challenge. A man in a suit reviews documents, with the word 'DIMENSION' appearing in bold yellow text, hinting at a new phase or perspective. The narrative builds to a moment of revelation: a man in a suit declares, 'LET'S CALL IT NIKE,' with the text appearing in bold yellow, followed by an explanation: 'IT'S THE GREEK WINGED GODDESS OF VICTORY,' as he gestures with conviction. The sequence ends with a close-up of a man in a suit, his expression intense, symbolizing the birth of a new brand and the beginning of a bold new chapter.

segments.caption: The segment opens with a tense boardroom scene, where the Man in Suit, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, stands before a group of executives, delivering a serious address. The camera cuts between close-ups of the Man in Suit and reactions from seated executives, including a man with gray hair and another with dark hair and a red tie, all displaying concern. The atmosphere is heavy, underscored by dim lighting and a large grandfather clock in the background. The Man in Suit continues, declaring, 'THIS IS THE MOMENT WE'VE BEEN WAITING FOR,' as animated icons—a purple delivery truck, a box, and a lightbulb—flash across the screen, symbolizing logistics, product, and innovation. The scene shifts to a group of men in suits, including one with glasses and a red tie, listening intently. The Man in Suit then appears in a dimly lit workshop, observing a Shoe Workshop Worker in a striped shirt and apron, operating a sewing machine, with another man in a red jacket overseeing the process. The setting suggests a shift from corporate strategy to hands-on production, emphasizing quality control and the physical labor behind the brand’s survival.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a legal document titled 'CUSTOM SERVICE' featuring a bold red line stating 'YOU OWE: $25 MILLION DOLLARS,' immediately establishing a high-stakes financial crisis. The scene shifts to a dimly lit office where a man in a black shirt appears distressed, followed by a man in a suit in a dark room, suggesting a tense corporate environment. The narrative then moves to a bustling office with employees packing boxes, indicating layoffs or restructuring, while a man in a light blue shirt walks through the space, reinforcing the sense of corporate upheaval. A woman is shown on a phone call by a window overlooking a cityscape, with the text 'THE BILL WAS COMPLETELY REAL' appearing, emphasizing the legitimacy of the financial threat. The camera then tilts up a towering skyscraper with the word 'SINISTER' in purple, heightening the ominous tone. In a dark boardroom, silhouetted figures discuss strategy, with the Keds and Converse logos appearing, followed by a man in a suit observing a computer screen displaying 'THE JAPAN THREAT,' suggesting a competitive business landscape. The scene transitions to a man in a suit in a glass-walled office, with a red 'STOP' sign overlay, symbolizing a halt to operations. Text overlays explain the 'ASP' rule—an archaic pre-Great Depression regulation—detailing that importers of similar shoes must pay a 20% duty based on a U.S. competitor’s price. The video concludes with a shot of a government building labeled 'NATIONALISM' and a man in a suit inspecting a vintage car, tying the financial and regulatory conflict to broader themes of American industry and protectionism.

segments.caption: The video opens with a dynamic, layered montage blending scenes of a man in a black Nike outfit crouching on a track, overlaid with a crowd of spectators, suggesting a public or athletic event. segments.caption: The segment opens with a title card reading 'CHAPTER 9: THE DARK TRUTH' in bold black text on a white background, flanked by purple sneaker sketches, setting a serious and investigative tone. The scene transitions to a dimly lit shoe factory where workers are engaged in production, with one man in a tan jacket holding a shoe, suggesting a focus on manufacturing. A man in a suit with a name tag 'BALLINGER' and a 'Warren' pin smiles against a backdrop of golden stars, possibly representing corporate leadership or a public figure. The narrative shifts to a sepia-toned historical scene of a man in a suit speaking into a microphone, followed by a group of uniformed individuals standing in formation in a dusty courtyard, with purple overlays of country names—Uganda, Bangladesh, Cambodia—indicating global labor issues. The visuals become increasingly grim, showing a dark, dilapidated interior with a toilet and shower icon, then a family icon with '100+' suggesting overcrowding. A woman in a military-style cap appears distressed, with red-circled icons of a water tap and toilet, emphasizing lack of sanitation. The scene cuts to a dark silhouette, then to a city skyline with '$1.25' in yellow, hinting at exploitative wages. A glitchy, purple-tinted graphic displays '2001', 'CAMBODIA', and '7 DAYS A WEEK, 16 HOURS A DAY', reinforcing the theme of extreme labor conditions. The segment ends with a red-tinted shot of a news anchor on a TV screen, with 'CNBC EXCLUSIVE' and 'MARIA BARTIROMO' visible, suggesting media exposure of these issues.

segments.caption: The video opens with bold purple text stating 'MAKE LESS THAN MINIMUM WAGE' and 'DENIED USE OF BATHROOMS AND DRINKING WATER,' immediately setting a tone of labor exploitation. The scene transitions to a man in a dark suit, identified as the Man in Suit, reading documents in an office, suggesting he is investigating these claims. A close-up of a computer screen reveals a news article from the Human Rights Resource Centre titled 'Human rights violations off the rack; Dutch and US brands allegedly rely on forced labor,' with highlighted text naming Nike, Patagonia, and C&A, and mentioning forced labor in China’s Xinjiang region. The Man in Suit is then shown in a serious conversation, gesturing with a pen, while another man in a blue shirt walks past a glass door labeled 'WASHOE MINING CORPORATION,' indicating a corporate setting. The tension escalates as the Man in Suit is confronted with the text 'THAT IS NOT AN EXCUSE!' in bold yellow letters. The scene shifts to a dimly lit, gritty environment where the Man in Suit appears distressed, followed by a chaotic outdoor scene of workers in a jungle-like setting, possibly a mining or factory camp, with makeshift structures and barrels. A close-up of a young man’s face, covered in dirt, conveys the harsh conditions. The video then cuts to a YouTube interface displaying various investigative videos, including 'The Dark Secrets Elizabeth Holmes Was Hiding' and 'The Untold Truth of Why Vine Died,' suggesting a theme of corporate exposés. The final frames show two men, one with binoculars and another in a cowboy hat, observing from a bridge, followed by a purple-tinted Nike logo with the text 'WHAT'S PARTICULARLY INTERESTING ABOUT NIKE,' and a man in a blue shirt standing over a massive pile of cash in a warehouse, symbolizing the financial stakes behind the labor issues.

segments.caption: The video opens with a dark, ominous shot of a man in a dark suit standing over a massive pile of cash in a warehouse, setting a tone of wealth and controversy. The scene transitions to a title card: 'CHAPTER 10: TURNING CONTROVERSY INTO MILLIONS,' overlaid on a cityscape with sneaker sketches, introducing the theme. The narrative then shifts to Nike's controversial ad campaign, 'Dream Crazy,' featuring Colin Kaepernick, shown smiling and then in a close-up with the tagline 'Believe in something. Even if it means sacrificing everything.' and the Nike slogan 'Just do it.' The video cuts to a news article titled 'Kneeling, Fiercely Debated in the N.F.L., Resonates in Protests,' showing Kaepernick kneeling during a game, linking the ad to his protest against racial injustice. A graphic of a raised fist appears with the text 'RACIAL INJUSTICE,' followed by a Fox News tweet quoting 'You cannot disrespect our country,' highlighting the backlash. The scene shifts to two men in suits walking and talking, with the word 'CONTROVERSIAL' in bold, then to a man in a plaid shirt and cap in a store, with 'STOCK PRICE IMMEDIATELY' appearing. A tweet shows a Nike sneaker being burned with the text 'Since when did the American Flag and the National Anthem become offensive?' The video ends with a blurred image of a hand holding a burning sneaker, overlaid with 'BUT THEN SOMETHING INTERESTING HAPPENED,' and a young man in a suit walking through a crowded office, suggesting a shift in public perception or business outcome.

segments.caption: The video opens with a split-screen: on the left, a close-up of Colin Kaepernick’s face with the Nike slogan 'Believe in something. Even if it means sacrificing everything.' and 'Just do it.' below, while on the right, a man in a dimly lit room looks down. Bold yellow text overlays state 'THEY WEIGHED UP THE COSTS AND BELIEVED THAT THE BENEFITS WERE WORTH IT.' The scene transitions to a man in a suit at a desk, then to a large, glitchy billboard in a Japanese city featuring Nike sneakers and Japanese text, with a red line cutting across it. Text appears: 'IN 1988 TO 1990' and 'BETWEEN WHEN THE FIRST JUST DO IT CAMPAIGN LAUNCHED,' followed by '$800 MILLION TO $9.2 BILLION' over a shot of people walking. The narrative shifts to a man in a suit in an office, then to a man in a suit on an airplane, looking tired. A woman in a flat cap appears with the text 'HIS STORY IS PRETTY BIGGEST.' The scene cuts to a man in a white shirt flexing in a car, then to a man in a suit at a diner, followed by a man in a dark suit in a dimly lit room. The video ends with the Nike logo and 'JUST DO IT.' on a dark background, then fades to black.
